text: I'm going to travel next month.
I'm going to learn the new system.
Where are you going to be in one year?
Next year I'm going to take the international accounts.

I worked at a small office.
I got a job at Gallagher.
I became a specialist.
Last year, I made the move.
Paula, hi, it's Greg. You have the big interview next Monday, right? I called to tell you one thing. When I started in this business, I was terrible.
You? Really? What happened?
My first month, a client asked me a simple question, and I froze. I said nothing for ten seconds. It felt like an hour.
So what did you do?
I took a breath, and I told him the truth. I said, I'm new, but I will find the answer today. And I did. I called three people, I found the number, and I sent it that afternoon.
And the client?
He stayed with me for ten years. He told me later he liked one thing: I did not guess. So here is my point, Paula.
Tell me.
Monday they will ask, what did you do? Do not give them a perfect person. Give them the real story: you began small, you worked, you became good. That is what they want.
Okay. I began small, I worked, I became good. I can say that.
You just said it. See you after the interview, Paula. You've got this.
I began my career at a small office.
I got a job at Gallagher and became a specialist.
I left Gallagher and went to Marsh.
Last year, I made the move to Zurich.
This time, the decision was mine.

Hi, Paula. David here. Before your lesson today, I want to tell you a story. It is my story, and it is short. Twenty years ago, I began in a tiny office in Ohio. I got a job as a junior clerk, and I made photocopies all day. I did not speak in meetings, because I was afraid. Then one day, a manager asked me a question in front of everyone, and I answered. It was small, but something changed. After that, I worked hard, I learned the business, and slowly I became a broker. A few years later, I left Ohio, I went to Miami, and last year I became a director. Here is the thing, Paula. For a long time, I thought my career just happened to me. Good luck, good timing, good people. But it did not just happen. I showed up. I answered the question. I made the moves. And today it is your turn. Tell your story, the real one, from your first office to Zurich. You did the work. Now say it out loud.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. Today you learned the past tense, and I want to say something about it, because it is bigger than grammar. For a year, I watched you tell your story in pieces, and you always said it the same way. They took me to Marsh. The clause closed the door. Zurich called me. Everything happened to you. Someone else always decided. But listen to the sentences you made in this lesson. I began. I got the job. I became a specialist. I left Gallagher. I went to Marsh. I made the move to Zurich. Do you hear it, Paula? In every sentence, you are the subject. You are the one who acts. That is not only grammar. That is the truth. Other people opened doors, yes, but you walked through them, and you did the hard work behind every one. Next Monday you have the Zurich interview, and they are going to ask you one question. What did you do? Do not give them a list of things that happened to you. Give them your story, in your own voice, with you at the start of every sentence. Because that is exactly where you belong. See you Monday, Paula.

I got a job at Gallagher and became a specialist.
